free_text: Interaction of 3- and 29-GeV Protons with Vanadium
Target foils were irradiated in the
circulating beam of the Brookhaven National
Laboratory (BNL) alternating gradient synchrotron.
35-cm3 Ge(Li) detector having a
resolution of 2.6 keV full width at half maximum at
1332 keV. A 6-in.-radius, 60 degree sector magnetic
deflection mass spectrometer equipped with an
extraction line made of Dom Corning 1720 glass.
A typical aligned stack consisted of one or two
25-microm-thick high-purity vanadium target foils
sandwiched symmetrically between two 25-microm
vanadium guard foils, two 12-microm aluminum guard
foils, two 25-microm aluminum monitor foils, and
finally two more 25-microm aluminum guard foils.
Decay curves mere constructed from the most
prominent gamma-ray peaks of each nuclide and the
analyses were done with the least-squares CLSQ code.
The errors in the radiochemical measurements
were calculated by root-mean square combinations of
the errors.
Radioactive counting.
Counting efficiency.
Branching ratios.
Cross section for production of 24Na from Al.
Activities at the end of bombardment mere corrected
for detector efficiency, gamma branching ratio,
summing of gamma rays (if any), and parent daughter
relationships.
BP
Irradiation times ranged from 10 sec to 5 h. Within
6-8 min after the end of the shortest bombardments
the irradiated vanadium foils were mounted on an
aluminum card and gamma counted.

These values are given as upper limits because low-
and medium- energy secondary protons generated in the
targets probably contribute appreciably to production
of these nuclides. This effect is most serious for the
(p,n) product, 51Cr.
